eli3: The context window is the maximum number of tokens a transformer can process in a single forward pass — both input tokens and any generated output tokens count toward this limit. GPT-2: 1024 tokens. GPT-3: 2048. GPT-4: 8K/32K/128K. Claude 3: 200K. Gemini 1.5 Pro: 1M. Beyond the context window, the model has no memory — earlier tokens are 'forgotten' unless retrieved externally.

The fundamental bottleneck is quadratic attention: self-attention computes an n×n attention matrix for a sequence of length n — memory is O(n²), compute is O(n²). For n=128K tokens: 128K² = 16 billion attention values — impractical naively. Long-context transformers solve this with: (1) efficient attention (FlashAttention, linear attention); (2) position encoding that generalizes (RoPE with scaling, ALiBi); (3) sliding window or sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird).

Long context windows enable new use cases: entire codebases as context, full legal documents, book-length summarization, multi-document reasoning. But long context ≠ reliable recall — the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon: models recall information near the beginning and end of long contexts well, but information in the middle is often missed. Effective context use requires both architectural and prompting strategies.

whenWhy.title: Document and codebase processing
whenWhy.body: With 128K+ context windows (GPT-4 Turbo, Claude 3), entire large files or small codebases fit in a single prompt. Use cases: (1) summarizing a 300-page report; (2) Q&A over a full legal contract; (3) code review of an entire module; (4) few-shot learning with many examples. These tasks were previously impossible with 2K-4K context models and required retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) as a workaround. Long context can replace RAG for moderate-sized corpora.

whenWhy.title: Multi-turn conversation memory
whenWhy.body: Conversational AI systems concatenate conversation history as the context. With 4K tokens: only ~20 turns of history. With 128K: hundreds of turns, or full conversation logs. This enables coherent long-form interactions without external memory management. However, the quadratic attention cost means long conversations are expensive at inference — each token generation costs O(n) attention over the full history. KV-cache mitigates this per step, but total context KV-cache grows with conversation length.

whenWhy.title: In-context learning with many examples
whenWhy.body: Larger context windows enable many-shot ICL (Agarwal et al. 2024): providing thousands of examples in the prompt. Many-shot ICL dramatically improves accuracy on hard tasks — GPT-4 with 2048 examples on a hate speech task: 90% accuracy vs 60% for zero-shot. Long context allows overriding model priors for specialized domains. This is a practical alternative to fine-tuning when labeled data is available but fine-tuning is infeasible.

deepDiveIntro: Standard attention complexity: Q, K, V ∈ ℝ^{n×d}. Computing QKᵀ: O(n²d) time, O(n²) memory (for the attention matrix). For n=128K, d=128, h=32 heads: attention matrix per head = 128K × 128K × 4 bytes = 64GB per head — clearly infeasible without approximation. FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022): fuse attention computation into tiles that fit in SRAM, avoid materializing the full O(n²) matrix — reduces memory to O(n), achieves exact attention with same numerical result.

Position encoding for long context: original BERT/GPT use absolute learned positions — cannot generalize beyond training length. RoPE (Rotary Position Embeddings): encode relative positions by rotating Q, K vectors. RoPE decays naturally with distance. Context extension with RoPE: YaRN (Peng et al. 2023) — apply a scaling factor to RoPE frequencies to extend beyond training length without degradation. ALiBi: linear attention bias −m·|i−j| generalizes to any length naturally.

deepDive.heading: FlashAttention for Efficient Long-Context Computation
deepDive.body: Standard attention: load Q, K, V from HBM (GPU RAM) → compute QKᵀ (O(n²)) → softmax → multiply by V → write output. I/O bottleneck: reading/writing the n×n attention matrix to HBM dominates runtime. FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022): split Q, K, V into blocks that fit in SRAM. Compute attention block-by-block using online softmax normalization (numerically stable). Never materialize the full n×n matrix. Memory: O(n). Speed: 2–4× faster than PyTorch native attention. FlashAttention-2 (2023): further optimizations for parallelism across sequence length. Standard in all modern LLM training. [1]
deepDive.formula: Memory: O(n²) → O(n) with FlashAttention; runtime: same O(n²d) FLOPs but IO-optimal

deepDive.heading: Position Encoding and Context Extension
deepDive.body: RoPE (Su et al. 2021): rotate Q and K vectors for each head at position m by angle mθₖ for dimension k. Dot product between positions m and n depends only on relative distance m−n → naturally position-relative. RoPE extrapolation degrades beyond training length. Extension methods: (1) Position interpolation (Chen et al. 2023): compress the position range to fit within training window by scaling positions; (2) YaRN (Peng et al. 2023): NTK-aware scaling + attention temperature adjustment — best generalization; (3) LongRoPE (Ding et al. 2024): non-uniform scaling per dimension. These enable extending Llama from 4K to 128K+ without full retraining. [2]

deepDive.heading: Lost in the Middle: Recall Degradation
deepDive.body: Liu et al. (2023) — 'Lost in the Middle': evaluated LLMs on multi-document QA requiring retrieval from a specific document within a long context. Finding: performance is highest when the relevant document is at the beginning or end of the context (primacy/recency effects), and drops significantly when it's in the middle. Example: GPT-3.5 on a 20-document context with relevant doc in middle: 35% accuracy; at start/end: 70–80%. Implication for RAG: put most relevant retrieved chunks at the beginning or end of the context, not in the middle. [3]

deepDive.heading: KV-Cache and Inference Cost at Long Context
deepDive.body: KV-cache: store key-value pairs for all past tokens to avoid recomputation. Memory: O(n·L·h·d_k) per sequence where n=context length, L=layers, h=heads, d_k=head dimension. For Llama 2 70B (L=80, h=64, d_k=128) at n=4096: 80×64×128×4096×2×2 bytes ≈ 10GB per sequence (fp16). At n=128K: ~320GB — exceeds single GPU VRAM. Solutions: quantized KV-cache (int4, int8), multi-query attention (MQA: share K,V across heads), grouped-query attention (GQA: group heads to share K,V) — Llama 2 uses GQA for long context. [2]

takeaways: Context window = max tokens in one forward pass; attention is O(n²) memory — the fundamental bottleneck. [1]
FlashAttention: tile-based computation, O(n) memory, 2–4× faster — standard in all modern LLMs. [1]
RoPE + YaRN: extend context beyond training length without full retraining. [2]
Lost in the middle: models recall beginning and end of context best — place key info at boundaries. [3]
KV-cache grows with context; MQA/GQA reduces memory for long-context inference. [2]

edgeCases.q: Does a longer context window always improve performance?
edgeCases.a: Not necessarily. (1) Lost in the middle: adding more context can hurt if the relevant information gets buried in the middle. (2) Distraction: irrelevant context in the prompt can confuse the model (Shi et al. 2023 — irrelevant information in math problems significantly hurts GPT-4's accuracy). (3) Cost: longer context means more expensive API calls and slower inference. (4) Out-of-distribution positions: models trained with RoPE interpolation may have degraded quality at the extreme end of their extended context (quality is best at ≤ original training length). The optimal strategy is often still RAG with selective retrieval — put the most relevant ~2K tokens in context, not a full 128K dump. [3]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between sparse attention (Longformer/BigBird) and full attention with FlashAttention?
edgeCases.a: Sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird): reduce the O(n²) FLOPs by computing attention only over a subset of token pairs. Longformer: local sliding window (size w) + a few global tokens — O(nw) attention. BigBird: local + global + random attention — O(n). Reduces both FLOPs and memory. Trade-off: misses long-range dependencies not captured by the sparse pattern. Full attention with FlashAttention: computes exact attention over all pairs — O(n²) FLOPs but I/O-optimized to O(n) memory. Preferred when n is moderate (up to 128K) and accuracy matters. For extreme lengths (millions of tokens), sparse or linear attention (Mamba SSMs) becomes necessary. [1]

sources.label: Dao et al. (2022) — FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness, NeurIPS 2022
Su et al. (2021) — RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
Liu et al. (2023) — Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts, TACL 2024
